title: Biweekly change in confirmed COVID-19 cases
subtitle: The biweekly change on any given date measures the percentage change in the number of new confirmed cases over the last 14 days, relative to the number in the previous 14 days. Due to limited testing, the number of confirmed cases is lower than the true number of infections.
caption:
Biweekly change in confirmed COVID-19 cases, Feb 22, 2026

The biweekly change on any given date measures the percentage change in the number of new confirmed cases over the last 14 days, relative to the number in the previous 14 days. Due to limited testing, the number of confirmed cases is lower than the true number of infections.

This choropleth map displays the biweekly percentage change in confirmed COVID-19 cases across the globe as of February 22, 2026. The color scale ranges from deep blue, representing a 100% decrease in cases, to deep red, representing a 100% increase, with hatched areas indicating no available data. The visual pattern shows a distinct geographic split, with the most significant declines concentrated in the Americas and parts of Europe, while a small cluster of increases appears in Central Europe. In South America, Peru and Bolivia are shaded in the darkest blue band, indicating a biweekly change between -75% and -100%. Neighboring countries show varying degrees of decline; Argentina and Uruguay fall into the -50% to -75% band, while Brazil and Colombia are in the lighter -25% to -50% range. In Central America and the Caribbean, Guatemala and Honduras also show declines in the -50% to -75% category. Across the Atlantic, the pattern is mixed. Most of Western and Southern Europe, including Spain, France, Italy, and the United Kingdom, shows a decrease in the -25% to -50% band. In contrast, a cluster of countries in Central and Eastern Europe, specifically Poland, the Czech Republic, and Slovakia, is shaded in light orange, placing them in the 0% to 25% increase band. Sweden and Norway show smaller increases in the same range. The vast majority of the African continent, along with Russia, China, and Australia, is covered in hatching, signifying that no data was reported for these regions during this period. The distribution of colors highlights a trend where the Americas and Southern Europe experienced falling case numbers, while a specific region in Central Europe saw a rise.
